figures.caption: Figure 1: Overview of the SIGNALCLAW framework. Each generation follows a Generate–Test–Evolve–Solidify loop. The LLM synthesizes candidate skills—structured artifacts comprising a strategy description, selection guidance, and executable code—conditioned on the current elite skill, performance metrics, and natural-language evolution signals extracted from traffic simulation. Signals such as high_queue or force_innovation are translated into targeted natural-language directions that steer the LLM toward semantically meaningful skill modifications.
Table 15: Event-injected scenario configurations. All scenarios use the same 4×4 arterial grid with 16 intersections and 3600 s simulation duration.

Table 18: Event-specialized skills used in event evaluation. Emergency, transit, and incident skills are evolved via dispatcher-context evolution (Section 4.4) with 30 generations of GPT-5.4. Emergency and incident skills converge to designs structurally similar to the initial seed, reflecting the narrow optimization landscape for rare-event response; the transit skill exhibits more substantial structural change (best at generation 9). The congestion skill is a hand-tuned rule (not evolved) since no dedicated congestion scenario is included in the evaluation. Event-context variables (e.g., emergency_distance) are injected into the sandbox alongside standard traffic features.

Table 17: Best evolved skills per routine scenario (full representation). Important: T1 is from the main 30-generation evolution used in all reported results (Tables 4 and 5). T2 and T3 are from separate extended 100-generation runs (same GPT-5.4 backbone, same setup as the ablation in Section 4.9) included solely to illustrate the diversity of evolved strategies across scenarios—these extended-run skills are not used in any evaluation table.

Figure 2: Overview of the twelve SUMO evaluation scenarios. (a) Routine scenarios use a 4×4 arterial grid with 16 signalized intersections; training (T1–T3) varies demand patterns while validation (V1–V3) perturbs demand by ±15%. (b) Emergency scenarios inject ambulances at regular intervals to test priority preemption. (c) Transit scenarios introduce bus lines requiring transit signal priority. (d) Incident and mixed scenarios simulate vehicle breakdowns and combined events to test compositional skill dispatch through the priority chain.

Table 12: Sandbox variable mapping. Abstract names are defined in the system prompt whitelist; lane-indexed names are aliases generated by the LLM. Both resolve to the same runtime value. Event-context variables (bottom block) have no lane prefix—they are global per intersection.

Table 5: End-to-end system comparison across event scenarios (mean ± std, 5 seeds). Best Avg Delay per scenario in bold; best event-specific delay (Emg/Bus) in bold. SignalClaw uses GPT-5.4-evolved event-specific skills with priority dispatch; baselines are trained/searched on T1–T3 only (zero-shot transfer to event scenarios). Avg Delay measures per-vehicle accumulated waiting time (seconds). Person-Delay in B1/B2 is per-person waiting time weighted by vehicle occupancy (bus ×30, car ×1.5) [Christofa et al., 2013], reflecting the societal cost of delaying high-occupancy vehicles; it aggregates all vehicle types, not buses alone. B1/B2 scenarios include dedicated bus lanes to distinguish from emergency scenarios.

Table 1: Comparison of SignalClaw with related TSC approaches. Prog.: strategies are interpretable programs. LLM: uses LLM for strategy synthesis. Signal: uses structured traffic feedback. Event: handles traffic events (EVP/TSP/incident). Multi-mode: supports multiple control modes. Audit: full evolution audit trail.

Table 9: Evolution of skills on T1. Each skill comprises a strategy description (natural language) and executable code. Skills progress from simple linear rules to conditional logic with multi-feature interactions. Generation 19 achieves the best fitness.

Table 4: Routine scenario comparison (mean ± std, 5 seeds). Avg Delay is per-vehicle accumulated waiting time (seconds). T1–T3 are training scenarios; V1–V3 are held-out validation scenarios with ±15% demand perturbation. Best per row in bold; worst adaptive method underlined.

Figure 3: Evolution curves across 30 generations. (a) Normal skill evolved on T1–T3: rapid improvement in the first 12 generations followed by a plateau (9.6% total improvement). (b) Event dispatcher-context evolution: transit skill achieves the fastest improvement (30.6% by generation 8), while emergency and incident skills show steady gains over more generations.
Table 13: Complete skill representation for the best T1 skill (generation 19). The three components—description, guidance, and executable code—form a self-documenting control artifact.
